video_title: The SaaSpocalypse Is Looking Like A Bank Run with Current's Stuart Sopp & Trevor Marshall
gist: Current co-founders Stuart Sopp (CEO) and Trevor Marshall (CTO) explain their $80M Series E at a $1.5B valuation framed as their last raise given profitability, then argue AI's near-term value is in infrastructure, business intelligence and biotech rather than incumbent SaaS or megacap banks. They liken the emerging 'SaaSpocalypse' to a self-reinforcing bank run on software vendors and read the US consumer as strained but with no recession warning signs.

insights.claim: Current raised an $80 million Series E at a $1.5 billion valuation led by new lead Spring Coast, framing it as the company's final raise because it is profitable on EBITDA/net income and will fund future growth from its own cash and balance sheet.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: Signals a profitable, capital-light fintech that no longer needs dilutive funding, a candidate for a strong IPO or M&A as the market reopens.
insights.quote: we raised $80 million series E at a valuation of 1.5 million

insights.claim: Current has grown over 70% for three consecutive years and deepened its relationship with issuing bank Cross River and General Catalyst's Customer Value Fund to finance its warehouse and marketing spend.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: Sustained 70%+ growth funded via warehouse/CVF structures rather than equity shows a durable unit-economics story underpinning any public-market valuation.
insights.quote: our growth rate which has been um over 70% uh for three consecutive years

insights.claim: Current positions itself as a net-new category combining banking with liquidity provision based on consumer trust, distinct from either pure banking or pure lending.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: Framing as a 'banking + liquidity' category, not a neobank, is how Current justifies premium growth and differentiates from commoditized challenger banks.
insights.quote: it's not banking on its own, it's not lending, it's really the combination of liquidity and banking

insights.claim: Money-center banks' cost to serve a consumer is above $400 a year, making low-balance underbanked customers a structurally losing value proposition for big banks even though they are profitable for Current.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: The ~$400/yr cost-to-serve gap is the structural moat letting fintechs profitably bank consumers that incumbents must abandon as banks tighten credit boxes.
insights.quote: the cost for the money center bank's cost to serve is around, you know, above $400 a year for most consumers

themes.theme: AI economics: tokens, models & cost discipline

insights.claim: Marshall argues Anthropic and OpenAI should worry because token serving, underlying infrastructure and model techniques (e.g. distilled models like DeepSeek) are getting far more efficient, so token prices keep falling even as providers invent novel ways to consume and price tokens.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: If per-token economics deflate structurally, frontier labs face margin pressure unless they continually invent higher-value, higher-consumption use cases.
insights.quote: if if I'm anthropic or open AI, I'm starting to get a little bit worried because like just the how you serve tokens and even like the infrastructure that it's running on is getting way more efficient

insights.claim: Marshall likens AI-compute cost optimization to Bitcoin mining's path from CPUs to GPUs to ASICs, predicting specialized chips and infrastructure will keep driving inference costs down for specific use cases.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: The ASIC analogy implies durable deflation in AI inference costs, favoring specialized-silicon players and pressuring general-purpose token pricing.
insights.quote: You saw that with Bitcoin... when A6 came out, it like crushed everything... that type of optimization is also happening for for AI compute

insights.claim: The 'token-maxing' era ended when CFOs and finance teams demanded measurable productivity for opex, with one company reportedly burning its annual AI budget in a quarter around the time of Uber's spend.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: Token usage as a vanity adoption metric is dead; AI spend is now gated by demonstrable ROI, capping near-term consumption growth for labs.
insights.quote: the bill got to the CFO and they were like... What's the measurable productivity?

insights.claim: Current is building architecture to hand off to lower-cost model versions, including self-hosting and tuning model weights for its use cases, because per-request AI costs of two or three cents add up across millions of users.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: Consumer-scale AI deployment forces aggressive cost-routing and self-hosting, a tell that high per-token pricing is unsustainable for mass-market apps.
insights.quote: three cents there, two cents here. This is going to add up a lot if we're putting it in front of millions of uh uh users

insights.claim: Sopp says the most underappreciated near-term AI value is business intelligence: once data sets are clean and classified, AI surfaces nonlinear second-, third- and fourth-order effects across a business that humans miss, which Current has used to find efficiencies in unexpected places.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: Points investors toward AI-driven internal BI/efficiency as a real ROI source today, versus speculative consumer-facing applications.
insights.quote: All of a sudden, AI can give you context on the things that are nonobvious. And so, that's really helpful for management

insights.claim: Marshall argues AI's winner is decided by distribution, not model quality, and that value will keep accreting to Google via Android, GCP, TPUs and Vertex unless Anthropic solves distribution itself.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: A distribution-over-model thesis favors Google's vertically integrated stack and treats standalone model labs as inputs that route through hyperscalers.
insights.quote: it ultim ultimately comes down to distribution um which is you know who is who's the one that's actually like

insights.claim: Current plans to route through Google's Vertex even when paying Anthropic and to host its own models on Vertex, illustrating how Google captures spend regardless of which model wins.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: Concrete proof that hyperscaler proxying lets Google monetize even Anthropic-served workloads, reinforcing the distribution-capture thesis.
insights.quote: even if we're not paying Gemini, we're probably routing through them in certainly in some form out to Anthropic

insights.claim: Sopp says AI is 'cloud 2.0,' a two-years-prior call from Marshall now validated, with Google stock having more than doubled from ~$160 and SpaceX entering cloud compute to monetize spare capacity into the IPO.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: The 'cloud 2.0' frame validates owning vertically integrated compute platforms and explains Google's re-rating and SpaceX's pivot to compute revenue.
insights.quote: two years ago, you said this is cloud 2.0 and it's proven out to be cloud 2.0

insights.claim: Sopp identifies biotech AI as the next monetization frontier beyond cloud compute, noting many AI founders are running side projects aimed at curing cancer and extending lifespan.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: Flags biotech-AI as where founder capital and attention are quietly flowing, a thematic watch area for the next AI investment wave.
insights.quote: biotech AI I think this is the frontier where I see a lot of the founders co-founders all doing their side projects clearly this is the monetization of AI

insights.claim: Marshall is skeptical of Microsoft Copilot uptake, noting Current uses Office for PowerPoint/Excel but pays Anthropic, and that Microsoft wins enterprise via bundling rather than product, with Copilot adoption reportedly under ~16-17% of its ~400M installed base.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: Weak Copilot attach on a huge base means either a large upsell runway or a product that can't justify higher per-seat pricing, a swing factor for Microsoft AI revenue.
insights.quote: we use it just because it's we like PowerPoint and Excel... we'll pay for productivity if we see it. um and we'll stop paying for it if we don't

insights.claim: Marshall suggests Microsoft should adopt Palantir's forward-deployed-engineer model to serve its older, cash-rich, government-tied customers who need help with data classification, unlike AI-native fintechs that extract value quickly themselves.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: Implies enterprise AI value depends on bespoke forward-deployment for legacy customers, advantaging services-heavy delivery models over self-serve platforms.
insights.quote: If I was them, I would take a leaf out of Panters's book for deploying engineers

insights.claim: Marshall frames forward-deployed engineering as an admission that AI products aren't yet sufficient platforms, making bespoke build-for-the-customer delivery more effective than an insufficient general platform for ~three-year-old AI sales companies.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: Helps investors read forward-deployed models as a maturity signal, not a moat, since robust platforms take years to build.
insights.quote: it's basically saying well we haven't built this like super platform so we're going to come in and tailor what we have to meet your needs

insights.claim: SpaceX/xAI, which was losing about a billion dollars a month, is leasing roughly 220,000 unused Nvidia GPUs to Anthropic and Google with ~90-day outs, because xAI's model isn't yet competitive while its rapid data-center buildout and local power give it a hardware edge.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: Idle GPUs leased on short notice hint at possible double/triple ordering and a loosening compute supply, raising obsolescence and demand-durability questions for the buildout.
insights.quote: they had 220,000 Nvidia GPUs that they were not using, which is pretty astounding

insights.claim: Marshall argues those who build their own data centers (vs. renting on 90-day-out contracts) are better positioned long-term, since xAI can reclaim leased capacity for its next model, making vertical compute ownership a structural advantage despite financing creativity.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: Renters of compute on short outs (e.g. Anthropic) carry capacity-clawback risk, favoring builders who own infrastructure outright.
insights.quote: it's definitely more precarious than building it yourself. So the people who have spent the time to build out data centers are probably better off over time

insights.claim: Sopp and Nathan note investors punish Meta more the more it spends on capex because Meta lacks a hyperscaler business to monetize excess compute and its own models 'suck,' so spend won't be rewarded until it shows ad-business revenue lift.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: Meta's capex gets penalized absent a compute-resale outlet or demonstrated ad ROI, a setup to watch for re-rating only once revenue inflects.
insights.quote: the more that they spend on capex is the more that investors punish them

insights.claim: Current keeps strong SaaS like Datadog that resells new tech better than building in-house, but is not renewing a long tail of SaaS it can now solve with lightweight internal tools, framing the 'SaaSpocalypse' as a self-reinforcing bank-run-like dynamic that threatens one-trick-pony vendors like Figma and Adobe.
insights.speaker: Trevor Marshall, co-founder/CTO Current
insights.so_what: AI displacing commoditized SaaS creates a recursive cancellation loop, favoring durable, complex/bespoke vendors and pressuring single-feature software stocks.
insights.quote: it's like a bank run. Yeah. On SaaS

insights.claim: Sopp says investors are hiding at the infrastructure, frontier-model and chip/ASIC layers because they cannot tell which application-layer SaaS survives AI disruption, while higher-complexity, more bespoke software is least likely to be replaced.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: Explains the crowding into infra/chips as a flight to safety amid application-layer uncertainty; complexity/bespokeness is the survival filter for software.
insights.quote: This is where why all the investors are hiding. they're hiding at the infrastructure level and at the frontier mod on the chips

insights.claim: Sopp reads the US consumer as strained but with no recession warning signs, calling the K-shaped narrative real but overstated, with inflation pressure concentrated in groceries and fuel for the $25-75K cohort that Current's liquidity products serve.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: A no-amber-warning consumer read supports continued demand for fintech liquidity products and argues against imminent recession positioning.
insights.quote: Honestly, there are no warning signs. There's not even like light amber. There's no amber yet

insights.claim: Sopp predicts the new Fed chair, a known hawk facing record gross Treasury issuance (~$500-600B/week) and White House pressure, will neither cut nor hike at his first Wednesday meeting but use words to talk the market down.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: Expect verbal jawboning rather than rate action at the first meeting, a near-term risk to rate-sensitive trades positioned for dovishness.
insights.quote: he's a known hawk... he's just going to be unchanged. But he does have that political pressure from from the White House and I think he's going to use some words to talk the market down

insights.claim: Sopp expects fintech to converge into roughly 10-20 big consumer fintech mega-caps over time, blending stablecoins, crypto and prediction markets with national vs. international distribution as Revolut and Nu Bank enter America and US fintechs go abroad.
insights.speaker: Stuart Sopp, co-founder/CEO Current
insights.so_what: A consolidation thesis pointing to a handful of fintech winners absorbing adjacent business models, a frame for picking long-term fintech survivors.
insights.quote: there's probably 10 maybe 20 big consumer fintech maybe, but it's just that that's what it will look like
